title: Female employment rate, 1980 vs. 2024
subtitle: Share of the working-age population who are employed. Figures refer to [national estimates](#dod:ilo-national-estimates) and may not be fully comparable across countries.
caption:
Female employment rate, 1980 vs. 2024
Share of the working-age population who are employed. Figures refer to national estimates and may not be fully comparable across countries.

This scatter plot compares the share of the working-age population employed in 1980 against the share in 2024 for a selection of countries, with circle size indicating total population. The horizontal axis represents the 2024 rate ranging from 0% to 100%, while the vertical axis represents the 1980 rate over the same range. A legend on the right assigns colors to regions: North America (orange), South America (maroon), Africa (purple), Europe (blue), Asia (teal), and Oceania (light blue). The data points generally fall below the diagonal line where 1980 and 2024 values would be equal, showing that most labeled nations have higher employment rates in 2024 than in 1980. The Bahamas sits furthest to the right with a 2024 rate near 90% and a 1980 rate around 25%. Nigeria displays a 2024 rate near 80% and a 1980 rate near 35%. Thailand and Burkina Faso occupy the upper portion of the chart with 1980 rates between 70% and 80%, while their 2024 rates sit between 55% and 60%. Malawi appears near the top left with a 1980 rate around 90% and a 2024 rate near 35%. A cluster of European and North American countries, including the United States, United Kingdom, Netherlands, and Brazil, groups in the center with 2024 rates between 50% and 60% and 1980 rates between 40% and 50%. Italy and Spain sit lower with 1980 rates near 30% and 2024 rates near 40% to 45%. Bangladesh and Pakistan show 2024 rates between 25% and 30% with 1980 rates near 10% to 15%. The United Arab Emirates sits near the bottom with a 1980 rate close to 0% and a 2024 rate near 50%. Employment is defined in line with the 13th ICLS and includes the production of goods for own use, such as subsistence farming. The visible pattern shows a broad shift toward higher female employment rates in 2024 compared to 1980 across the majority of labeled countries.
